def find_cycle_in_directed_graph(graph):
    """
    Detects if there is a cycle in a directed graph.

    Args:
        graph: A dictionary representing the directed graph using an adjacency list.
               Keys are nodes, values are lists of their neighbors.

    Returns:
        True if a cycle is detected, False otherwise.
    """
    
    # Collect all unique nodes from keys and values to ensure comprehensive checking
    all_nodes = set(graph.keys())
    for neighbors in graph.values():
        all_nodes.update(neighbors)

    # State definitions:
    # 0: Unvisited - Node has not been visited yet.
    # 1: Visiting - Node is currently in the recursion stack (part of the current DFS path).
    # 2: Visited - Node has been fully processed, and all its descendants have been visited.
    node_states = {node: 0 for node in all_nodes}

    def dfs(node):
        node_states[node] = 1  # Mark node as 'Visiting'

        # Iterate over all neighbors of the current node
        for neighbor in graph.get(node, []):
            if node_states[neighbor] == 1:
                # If neighbor is 'Visiting', a back-edge is found, meaning a cycle exists.
                return True
            if node_states[neighbor] == 0:
                # If neighbor is 'Unvisited', recursively call DFS on it.
                if dfs(neighbor):
                    return True  # A cycle was found in the sub-graph rooted at neighbor.
        
        node_states[node] = 2  # Mark node as 'Visited' (finished processing)
        return False

    # Iterate through all nodes to ensure all connected components are checked.
    # This is crucial for disconnected graphs.
    for node in all_nodes:
        if node_states[node] == 0:  # If the node has not been visited yet, start a new DFS.
            if dfs(node):
                return True  # A cycle was found in this component.

    return False  # No cycles found in any component.
